svg.text: HAWAIIAN + ENGLISH — SCRIPT · WRITING SYSTEMS
ALT DRAFT · SERIES 59A · REMEMBER FORWARD · EXPANDED ~2500MI RADIUS
— HAWAIIAN — LATIN ALPHABET · 13 LETTERS —
Hawaiian uses only 13 letters: 5 vowels (A E I O U) and 8 consonants (H K L M N P W ʻ).
One of the smallest alphabets of any natural language. Every syllable is consonant-vowel or vowel alone.
ʻOkina (ʻ): a letter — the glottal stop /ʔ/. Visually resembles an apostrophe but functions as a consonant.
— HAWAIIAN ALPHABET — ALL 13 LETTERS —
A E I O U H K L M N P W ʻ
Every syllable = (C)V. No consonant clusters. No final consonants. Language sounds deeply open/vowel-rich.
— KAHAKŌ (MACRON) — VOWEL LENGTH —
The kahakŌ (ā ē ī ō ū) marks long vowels — a distinct phoneme from the short version.
kāne (man) vs. kane (name) · ʻala (path) vs. ʻala (to bark) · moa (chicken) vs. mōa (used up).
Both kahakŌ and ʻokina are official letters in the Hawaiian alphabet since 1978 (not decorations).
— HAWAIIAN WRITING HISTORY —
Hawaiian had no writing system before 1820s when Protestant missionaries developed the Latin orthography.
Ka Nupepa Kuokoa (1861–1927): Hawaiian-language newspaper with millions of words of native text.
1896: Hawaiian language banned from public schools by territorial government. Near extinction followed.
1978: Hawaiian becomes official state language. Pūnana Leo immersion schools founded 1984. Revitalization active.
— ENDANGERED STATUS — REVITALIZATION —
~2,000 native speakers (~1990s low). Now: ~24,000 speakers including second-language learners.
Pūnana Leo (Language Nest) schools: immersion kindergartens; entire curriculum in Hawaiian.
Online resources, university programs, and state law now support Hawaiian language use.
SECONDARY — ENGLISH — SCRIPT · WRITING SYSTEM · 2500MI RADIUS
— ENGLISH — LATIN ALPHABET · 26 LETTERS —
English uses the standard 26-letter Latin alphabet, inherited from Old English via Latin/Norman French.
English spelling is notoriously irregular: through, though, thought, tough, cough — all different pronunciations of -ough.
Great Vowel Shift (~1400–1700 CE): all long vowels changed. Spelling was frozen before the shift ended.
— ENGLISH WRITING HISTORY —
Old English (Anglo-Saxon): originally written in runic Futhorc, then Latin alphabet after Christianization (~600 CE).
Norman Conquest (1066): French scribal conventions overlaid on English — qu- for cw-, c for k before e/i.
Caxton’s printing press (1476): standardized spelling from the London/East Midlands dialect.
— SCRIPT BRIDGE — THE MEETING OF SCRIPTS IN HAWAIʻiI —
Hawaiian was given the Latin alphabet by English-speaking missionaries in 1822 — a colonial imposition.
Yet Hawaiian speakers embraced writing: ka nupepa (newspapers) became central to cultural expression.
Today English and Hawaiian share the Latin alphabet but use it for very different phonological systems.
13 letters vs. 26. Open syllables vs. complex clusters. Phonemic vowel length vs. variable English stress.
